commonIssues: Frost-tender annual in most climates (perennial only in USDA zones 10-11); plant outdoors only after the last spring frost and soil reaches about 16C (60F).
Needs full sun (6+ hours); becomes spindly with few flowers in shade.
Container and hanging-basket plants dry out fast and may need daily watering in hot weather, but require well-drained mix as roots are prone to rot if waterlogged.
Container minimum (5 L) reflects typical commercial 1.25-1.5 gal hanging baskets; larger pots (8-12 L) hold more soil, dry slower, and support fuller trailing displays.
Aphids, slugs/snails, flea beetles, and caterpillars can occur; petal blight, gray mold (botrytis), root/stem rot, and tobacco mosaic virus are possible in wet or humid conditions.
Deadhead spent blooms to keep flowering through the season.

source.citation: University of Minnesota Extension — Growing petunias. Full sun (5-6+ hours); thorough watering once a week in ground, containers/hanging baskets as often as daily; containers fed every 2 weeks with liquid fertilizer from early/mid-July; transplant after frost danger and soil ~60F (16C); spreading types trail 2-3 ft.
University of Minnesota Extension — Growing petunias. Balanced garden fertilizer 8-8-8/10-10-10/12-12-12; containers fed every 2 weeks; spreading/container types need more frequent feeding.
University of Minnesota Extension — Growing petunias. Petunias need at least 5-6 hours of good sunlight and perform better in full sun all day.
University of Minnesota Extension — Growing petunias. Transplant only after frost danger passes and soil warms to about 60F (16C); seedling growing temps ~65F day / 55-65F night.

University of Florida IFAS Gardening Solutions — Petunias. At least 1-2 inches of water every 7-10 days once established; avoid frequent shallow watering; fertilize monthly with balanced or controlled-release fertilizer; well-drained soil kept moist; aphids, caterpillars, petal blight in humid weather.

Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC — Petunia. Plant in full sun (spindly with few flowers in shade); 1-2 inches water every 7-10 days once established; fertilize monthly with balanced fertilizer; soil pH 6.0-7.0; aphids, slugs, petal blight, viruses.
Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC — Petunia. Full sunlight required; bloom from spring until frost; plant as soon as spring frost danger is past.

NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — Petunia x hybrida. USDA hardiness zones 10a-11b (perennial; grown as annual elsewhere); full sun 6+ hours or partial shade 2-6 hours; height 6-18 in, width 1-3 ft; spreading/trailing habit; problems include aphids, flea beetles, slugs/snails, root rot, gray mold, tobacco mosaic virus.

NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — Petunia x hybrida. Spreading/trailing/horizontal growth habit (width 1-3 ft), widely grown in hanging baskets and as a trailing groundcover — supports high vertical/hanging suitability.

Container size estimated, not given as liters by extension sources. Commercial petunia hanging baskets are typically 1.25-1.5 gal (~5-6 L); a 12-in/#10 hanging basket holds ~5.7 L. recommendedMinLiters set to 5 L (realistic commercial minimum), idealMin/Max 8-12 L for fuller trailing displays and slower drying. Sources: Proven Winners petunia hanging-basket guidance and standard nursery container sizing.

Low-confidence fields. containerProfile liters are converted from typical commercial basket sizes (extension sources specify basket diameter / plant counts, not soil volume). heatStressC (32C) and coldHardyMinC (15C) are conservative estimates derived from UMN transplant guidance (soil ~16C/60F) and general extension notes that petunias prefer ~18-27C and are frost-tender; extension pages do not state an exact heat-stress threshold. hotWeatherMultiplier and rainSkipThresholdMm are app-tuned conservative defaults consistent with 'water as often as daily in heat' and 1-2 in/week guidance.
